# Challenges and directions in studying cell–cell communication by extracellular vesicles

[*Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology*](/nrm)
**volume 23**, pages 369–382 (2022)[Cite this article](#citeas)

40k Accesses

860 Citations

130 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s41580-022-00460-3/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

Extracellular vesicles (EVs) are increasingly recognized as important mediators of intercellular communication. They have important roles in numerous physiological and pathological processes, and show considerable promise as novel biomarkers of disease, as therapeutic agents and as drug delivery vehicles. Intriguingly, however, understanding of the cellular and molecular mechanisms that govern the many observed functions of EVs remains far from comprehensive, at least partly due to technical challenges in working with these small messengers. Here, we highlight areas of consensus as well as contentious issues in our understanding of the intracellular and intercellular journey of EVs: from biogenesis, release and dynamics in the extracellular space, to interaction with and uptake by recipient cells. We define knowledge gaps, identify key questions and challenges, and make recommendations on how to address these.

# A review of the regulatory mechanisms of extracellular vesicles-mediated intercellular communication

[*Cell Communication and Signaling*](/)
**volume 21**, Article number: 77 (2023)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

18k Accesses

192 Citations

12 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/10.1186/s12964-023-01103-6/metrics)

## Abstract

Extracellular vesicles (EVs) are small, membrane-bound structures that are released from cells into the surrounding environment. These structures can be categorized as exosomes, microvesicles, or apoptotic vesicles, and they play an essential role in intercellular communication. These vesicles are attracting significant clinical interest as they offer the potential for drug delivery, disease diagnosis, and therapeutic intervention. To fully understand the regulation of intercellular communication through EVs, it is essential to investigate the underlying mechanisms. This review aims to provide a summary of the current knowledge on the intercellular communications involved in EV targeting, binding, and uptake, as well as the factors that influence these interactions. These factors include the properties of the EVs, the cellular environment, and the recipient cell. As the field of EV-related intercellular communication continues to expand and techniques improve, we can expect to uncover more information about this complex area, despite the current limitations in our knowledge.

## Background

Extracellular vesicles (EVs) play a critical role in mediating and regulating intercellular communication associated with both physiological and pathological processes [[1](#ref-CR1 "Kalluri R, LeBleu VS. The biology, function, and biomedical applications of exosomes. Science. 2020;367(6478):eaau6977."),[2](#ref-CR2 "Van Niel G, d’Angelo G, Raposo G. A brief history of nearly EV-erything – The rise and rise of extracellular vesicles. J Extracell Vesicles. 2021;10(14):e12144.")]. EVs (Fig. [1](/articles/10.1186/s12964-023-01103-6#Fig1)) are lipid membranous vesicles consisting of proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids, which are heterogenous associated with the composition and function [[5](/articles/10.1186/s12964-023-01103-6#ref-CR5 "Théry C, et al. Minimal information for studies of extracellular vesicles 2018 (MISEV2018): a position statement of the International Society for Extracellular Vesicles and update of the MISEV2014 guidelines. J Extracell Vesicles. 2018;7(1):1535750.")]. EVs can be classified into several types based on their origin, such as exosomes, microvesicles, and apoptotic vesicles [[2](/articles/10.1186/s12964-023-01103-6#ref-CR2 "Van Niel G, d’Angelo G, Raposo G. Shedding light on the cell biology of extracellular vesicles. Nat Rev Mol Cell Biol. 2018;19(4):213–28."), [5](/articles/10.1186/s12964-023-01103-6#ref-CR5 "Théry C, et al. Minimal information for studies of extracellular vesicles 2018 (MISEV2018): a position statement of the International Society for Extracellular Vesicles and update of the MISEV2014 guidelines. J Extracell Vesicles. 2018;7(1):1535750.")]. Exosomes, which range in size from 30–150 nm, are secreted within the multivesicular endosomes (MVEs), fused with the cell surface, and then released, making them intermediates within the endosomal system [[6](/articles/10.1186/s12964-023-01103-6#ref-CR6 "Colombo M, Raposo G, Thery C. Biogenesis, secretion, and intercellular interactions of exosomes and other extracellular vesicles. Annu Rev Cell Dev Biol. 2014;30:255–89."), [7](/articles/10.1186/s12964-023-01103-6#ref-CR7 "Kalluri R, LeBleu VS. The biology, function, and biomedical applications of exosomes. Science,. 2020;367(6478):eaau6977.")]. Different from exosomes, microvesicles (50–1000 nm) are secreted directly through ectocytosis, which allows the release of plasma membrane vesicles [[8](/articles/10.1186/s12964-023-01103-6#ref-CR8 "Muralidharan-Chari V, et al. Microvesicles: mediators of extracellular communication during cancer progression. J Cell Sci. 2010;123(10):1603–11."), [9](/articles/10.1186/s12964-023-01103-6#ref-CR9 "Stahl PD, Raposo G. Extracellular Vesicles: Exosomes and Microvesicles. Integrators Homeostasis Physiol. 2019;34(3):169–77.")]. The key difference between microvesicles and exosomes is that the intracellular membrane is not involved during the secretion of microvesicles [[2](/articles/10.1186/s12964-023-01103-6#ref-CR2 "Van Niel G, d’Angelo G, Raposo G. Shedding light on the cell biology of extracellular vesicles. Nat Rev Mol Cell Biol. 2018;19(4):213–28.")]. In contrast, apoptotic bodies are typically larger with a size from 1–5 μm, which are generated during the process of programmed cell death [[10](/articles/10.1186/s12964-023-01103-6#ref-CR10 "Battistelli M, Falcieri E. Apoptotic Bodies: Particular Extracellular Vesicles Involved in Intercellular Communication. Biology. 2020;9(1):21.")]. The general introductions of EVs, including the compositions, the related physiological and pathological processes, and their clinical applications. EVs are lipid membranous vesicles. The communication based on EVs is related to their surface proteins and their cargoes including protein, lipid, and nucleic acids. EVs participate in diverse biological processes, such as cell motility, differentiation, proliferation, apoptosis, reprogramming, waste management, metastasis, and inflammation. The biological processes related to EVs are linked with their clinical potentials such as diagnosis based on their biomarker properties, drug delivery with their targeting properties, and targeting therapy using their communication properties

## Targeting, binding, and uptake of EVs

### Targeting

There are two questions related to the targeting orientation of exosomes toward the cell, including (1) Are there any signal compounds that determine the direction of exosomes in the biological environment? There is no clear evidence showing that any signal compounds lead to the movement of exosomes toward any organ or a specific type of cell [[55](/articles/10.1186/s12964-023-01103-6#ref-CR55 "Mulcahy LA, Pink RC, Carter DRF. Routes and mechanisms of extracellular vesicle uptake. J Extracell Vesicles. 2014;3(1):24641."), [70](/articles/10.1186/s12964-023-01103-6#ref-CR70 "Wiklander OPB, et al. Extracellular vesicle in vivo biodistribution is determined by cell source, route of administration and targeting. J Extracell Vesicles. 2015;4(1):26316.")]. Recent studies have shown that exosomes can be non-selectively incorporated into various types of recipient cells [[71](/articles/10.1186/s12964-023-01103-6#ref-CR71 "Horibe S, et al. Mechanism of recipient cell-dependent differences in exosome uptake. BMC Cancer. 2018;18(1):47.")]. However, another study shows that there is a possibility that cellular targeting is based on signaling molecules such as morphogen, with a source-sink mechanism being suggested [[72](/articles/10.1186/s12964-023-01103-6#ref-CR72 "Takahashi Y, et al. Visualization and in vivo tracking of the exosomes of murine melanoma B16-BL6 cells in mice after intravenous injection. J Biotechnol. 2013;165(2):77–84.")]. Indeed, a biodistribution study shows that red blood cell-derived EVs predominantly target the liver and bone [[73](/articles/10.1186/s12964-023-01103-6#ref-CR73 "Yáñez-Mó M, et al. Biological properties of extracellular vesicles and their physiological functions. J Extracell Vesicles. 2015;4(1):27066.")]. Also, melanoma-derived EVs were mainly taken up by the lungs and spleen [[72](/articles/10.1186/s12964-023-01103-6#ref-CR72 "Takahashi Y, et al. This cell-type-specificity targeting may be related to receptor-ligand binding, which leads to the second question. # On the road: extracellular vesicles in intercellular communication

[*Cell Communication and Signaling*](/)
**volume 23**, Article number: 95 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

1273 Accesses

6 Citations

[Metrics details](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8/metrics)

## Abstract

Cells from organisms across all kingdoms of life continuously release a diverse repertoire of extracellular vesicles (EVs) into their extracellular environment as an elegant strategy for both, cellular homeostasis and communication with other cells. Through different biogenesis routes within the donor cell, nanosized vesicles are generated either from endomembranes or the plasma membrane, and loaded and decorated with macromolecular cargo in a controlled manner through molecular sorting machineries. Since they can affect a recipient cell in the same tissue, distant organs or even other organisms, EVs have been increasingly recognized as essential mediators orchestrating intercellular communication in health and disease. In the last 15 years, research on the fundamental biology of EVs as well as their potential for biomedical applications has been greatly intensified. Time to present new advances on EV biogenesis, their intercellular communication competencies as well as technical and biomedical applications in a special thematic series of *Cell Communication and Signaling*.

## Introduction

The discovery of extracellular vesicles (EVs) can be traced back at least to the 1940ies when, during their studies on blood coagulation, Chargaff and West reported the observation of ‘a variety of minute breakdown products of blood corpuscles’ with thromboplastic function [[1](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR1 "Chargaff E, West R. The biological significance of the thromboplastic protein of blood. J Biol Chem. 1946;166:189–97.")]. It took almost 20 years until these breakdown products were further characterized; based on their appearance in the electron microscope and data demonstrating that they originate from platelets in a study led by Peter Wolf [[2](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR2 "Wolf P. The nature and significance of platelet products in human plasma. Br J Haematol. 1967;13:269–88.")]. They were described as ‘platelet dust’ with an initially hypothesized function in cellular waste removal. Subsequent studies by Neville Crawford and coworkers then further characterized these as platelet derived ‘microparticles’ which comprise lipids, ATP and contractile proteins, thereby hinting towards a potential functional role [[3](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR3 "Crawford N. The presence of contractile proteins in platelet microparticles isolated from human and animal platelet-free plasma. Br J Haematol. 1971;21:53–69.")]. In parallel, independent research by virologists during the late 1950ies to 1970ies yielded observations of virus-like particles in body fluids from cancer patients that were not of ‘viral nature’ [[4](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR4 "Levine PH, Horoszewicz JS, Grace JT Jr., Chai LS, Ellison RR, Holland JF. Relationship between clinical status of leukemic patients and virus-like particles in their plasma. Cancer. 1967;20:1563–77."), [5](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR5 "Seman G, Gallager HS, Lukeman JM, Dmochowski L. Studies on the presence of particles resembling RNA virus particles in human breast tumors, pleural effusions, their tissue cultures, and milk. Cancer. 1971;28:1431–42.")], which resulted in much controversy on their biological origin and relevance [[6](#ref-CR6 "Fawcett DW. Electron microscope observations on intracellular virus-like particles associated with the cells of the Lucké renal adenocarcinoma. J Biophys Biochem Cytol. 1956;2:725–41."),[7](#ref-CR7 "Lunger PD, Lucas JC Jr., Shipkey FH. THE ULTRAMORPHOLOGY OF MILK FRACTIONS FOR NORMAL AND BREAST CANCER PATIENTS. A PRELIMINARY REPORT. Cancer. 1964;17:549–57."),[8](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR8 "Prince AM, Adams WR. Virus-like particles in human plasma and serum: role of platelet lysosomes. J Natl Cancer Inst. 1966;37:153–66.")]. Interest in these virus-like extracellular particles was finally fading out after a study by Dalton and coworkers concluded them to be a result of technical artefacts, since similar particles were identified in serum containing medium irrespective of whether the medium had been conditioned by cells or not, thus questioning their cellular origin [[9](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR9 "Dalton AJ. Microvesicles and vesicles of multivesicular bodies versus virus-like particles. J Natl Cancer Inst. 1975;54:1137–48.")]. A breakthrough in the field was finally achieved when multivesicular bodies (MVBs) were first identified by Nunez et al. in 1974 in an electron microscopy study of the bat thyroid gland after arousal from hibernation [[10](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR10 "Nunez EA, Wallis J, Gershon MD. Secretory processes in follicular cells of the bat thyroid. 3. The occurrence of extracellular vesicles and colloid droplets during arousal from hibernation. Am J Anat. 1974;141:179–201.")]. Since these vesicle-filled enveloped bags were localized close to the apical surface within the cells, and since small vesicle-like structures of 1–10 nm were additionally captured in the extracellular environment in vicinity to these MVBs, this led the authors to hypothesize on a controlled production and release of ‘exosomes’ by fusion of the MVB with the plasma membrane. This not only turned out to be true but paved the way for many studies to follow on the biogenesis and biological function of exosomes and other types of, non-MVB derived, EVs.

Since these first pioneering experiments, there has been enormous research progress in the field. The interest further exploded after two independent landmark studies were published by the groups of Jan Lötvall in 2007 and Matthew Wood in 2011, which first revealed that EVs can transfer small RNAs across cells [[11](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR11 "Valadi H, Ekström K, Bossios A, Sjöstrand M, Lee JJ, Lötvall JO. Exosome-mediated transfer of mRNAs and microRNAs is a novel mechanism of genetic exchange between cells. Nat Cell Biol. 2007;9:654–9.")] and pioneering the idea to hijack this system for targeted delivery of exogenous, therapeutic siRNA [[12](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR12 "Alvarez-Erviti L, Seow Y, Yin H, Betts C, Lakhal S, Wood MJ. Delivery of siRNA to the mouse brain by systemic injection of targeted exosomes. Nat Biotechnol. 2011;29:341–5.")]. Remarkably, to date more than 10,000 publications on EVs are listed in PubMed since June 2023 and numerous companies have been founded across the health care and technology industry, underlining both, the fundamental role of EVs in living organisms and the high level of expectations on their potential for biomedical applications.

Based on current understanding, mammalian cells continuously release multiple different types of extracellular vesicles including, based on their biogenesis route, plasma membrane derived microvesicles (MVs), MVB derived exosomes, as well as apoptotic bodies. In addition to these major classes, the continuous discovery of either new or specialized types of EVs which are defined by e.g. their cell of origin, condition-dependent secretion or sub-classifications based on parameters like size, density or specific markers has greatly contributed to ambiguous and overlapping nomenclature, and much effort is being invested in the field to establish a uniform, consensus terminology. Nanosized vesicles are also secreted by lower organisms, including for example outer membrane vesicles (OMVs), originating from the outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria, or bacterial EVs (BEVs) from Gram-positive bacteria. Released EVs can mediate paracrine and endocrine communication with cells by interacting with cells in their immediate environment, but can also be localized to more distant sites in the body [[13](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR13 "Kita S, Shimomura I. Extracellular Vesicles as an Endocrine Mechanism Connecting Distant Cells. Mol Cells. 2022;45:771–80.")] where they exhibit multifaceted possibilities to interact with recipient cells influencing cellular responses and subsequently the physiology of the organism. Intensive research has been carried out to investigate the biogenesis mechanisms, sorting, packaging and content of EVs, which encompasses proteins and glycoproteins, DNA, RNA, lipids and metabolites [[14](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR14 "Doyle LM, Wang MZ. Overview of Extracellular Vesicles, Their Origin, Composition, Purpose, and Methods for Exosome Isolation and Analysis. Cells 2019, 8."), [15](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR15 "Lee YJ, Shin KJ, Chae YC. Regulation of cargo selection in exosome biogenesis and its biomedical applications in cancer. Exp Mol Med. 2024;56:877–89.")]. Since the molecular composition of each individual EV links information on the identity with the physiological state of the parent cell within the same particle, and since these particles can be easily accessed within most body fluids, EVs are now considered highly attractive, minimally invasive (prognostic) biomarkers [[16](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR16 "Ciferri MC, Quarto R, Tasso R. Extracellular Vesicles as Biomarkers and Therapeutic Tools: From Pre-Clinical to Clinical Applications. Biology (Basel) 2021, 10.")]. Additionally, their role in mediating cellular cues not only to neighboring cells but also distant cells in other tissues and even across organisms makes them promising therapeutic candidates, such as for cell-free cell therapy. Finally, their efficient penetration across biological barrier with a potential for inherent tropism and active internalization by recipient cells has triggered high expectations of their potential as drug delivery vehicles. As much as the interest in the biology of EVs and their potential for biomedical applications has exploded in the last decade, debates in the field increasingly gravitate around a number of fundamental questions which have turned out to remain elusive; it has been repeatedly demonstrated that many cells take up EVs from many sources with high efficiency when they are exposed to them. Since uptake therefore does not appear to be as specific and limiting as initially thought, how then is specificity achieved? Further, given that EVs are primarily internalized as intact vesicles to shuttle within endosomes [[17](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR17 "Heusermann W, Hean J, Trojer D, Steib E, von Bueren S, Graff-Meyer A, Genoud C, Martin K, Pizzato N, Voshol J, et al. Exosomes surf on filopodia to enter cells at endocytic hot spots, traffic within endosomes, and are targeted to the ER. J Cell Biol. 2016;213:173–84.")], it remains enigmatic whether, how and to what extent nucleic acid or protein cargo really is released to access molecular targets in the right localization and at meaningful concentrations within the recipient cells. And while the beautifully diverse composition of EVs provides for powerful effects due to combinatorial mechanisms, linking biological activities to clearly defined molecular mechanisms and dissecting cause versus consequence has turned out as a major challenge. In light of this and several other basic questions in the field, this thematic series covers review as well as original research articles summarizing current knowledge and to expand it with new examples of EV-mediated intercellular communication in physiological processes as well as chronic, acute and infectious diseases.

## The collection of articles within the thematic series ‘Intercellular communication by extracellular vesicles in health and disease’

Our collection consists of 20 research articles and reviews, which are briefly summarized as follows:

**Reviews**:

In a review article, Razizadeh and colleagues summarizes the recent knowledge about EVs, and in particular exosomes, released by natural killer (NK) cells, their roles in viral infections as well as their potential as antiviral therapeutic agents [[18](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR18 "Razizadeh MH, Zafarani A, Taghavi-Farahabadi M, Khorramdelazad H, Minaeian S, Mahmoudi M. Natural killer cells and their exosomes in viral infections and related therapeutic approaches: where are we? Cell Commun Signal. 2023;21:261.")].

In a comprehensive overview on the different types of apoptotic vesicles and their medical applications, Zou et al. describe the features and biogenesis of membrane-bound apoptotic bodies, apoptotic microvesicles and apoptotic exosomes from different cell types. The broad spectrum of therapeutic applications of apoptotic vesicles along with the advantages and disadvantages or limits have been addressed [[19](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR19 "Zou X, Lei Q, Luo X, Yin J, Chen S, Hao C, Shiyu L, Ma D. Advances in biological functions and applications of apoptotic vesicles. Cell Commun Signal. 2023;21:260.")].

The review article by Dong et al. discussed extracellular vesicles in the context of traumatic brain injury (TBI). They summarize the literature on pathological EVs (PEV), biological EVs (BEV), and drug-loaded engineered special purpose EVs (EEV) as vesicle subtypes that may have different effects on pathophysiological process of secondary damage from TBI, progression of TBI secondary damage, tissue repair and body rehabilitation, and on treatments of TBI [[20](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR20 "Dong X, Dong JF, Zhang J. Roles and therapeutic potential of different extracellular vesicle subtypes on traumatic brain injury. Cell Commun Signal. 2023;21:211.")].

Fu and colleagues review how tumor-derived extracellular vesicles (TDEs) influence tumor and host metabolism. TDEs alter glucose, lipid, and amino acid metabolism, contributing to tumor progression by creating a supportive microenvironment. The review highlights TDEs’ role in immune system modulation, their potential as biomarkers for early diagnosis, and perspectives on their use as drug delivery systems to offer new strategies for cancer treatment [[21](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR21 "Fu X, Song J, Yan W, Downs BM, Wang W, Li J. The biological function of tumor-derived extracellular vesicles on metabolism. Cell Commun Signal. 2023;21:150.")].

In this review, the authors summarize the dual roles of EVs, and in particular exosomes, in the context of the blood-brain barrier (BBB). The review summarizes recent publication how exosomes can both support the integrity of the BBB under physiological conditions and contribute to its disruption in pathological conditions. It discusses the biogenesis of exosomes, isolation methods and their potential as therapeutic agents for BBB drug delivery in the treatment of neurological diseases [[22](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR22 "Salimi L, Seyedaghamiri F, Karimipour M, Mobarak H, Mardi N, Taghavi M, Rahbarghazi R. Physiological and pathological consequences of exosomes at the blood-brain-barrier interface. Cell Commun Signal. 2023;21:118.")].

Migrasomes are extracellular vesicles discovered relatively recently which are formed at the tips of retraction fibers during cell migration. Their role in intercellular communication by carrying and releasing signaling molecules like cytokines, chemokines, and growth factors, which can influence the behavior of neighboring cells has here been reviewed by Jiang and colleagues. They describe the biogenesis and structure of migrosomes, techniques for the isolation and their functional role in positioning and behavior of other cells during cell migration [[23](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR23 "Jiang Y, Liu X, Ye J, Ma Y, Mao J, Feng D, Wang X. Migrasomes, a new mode of intercellular communication. Cell Commun Signal. 2023;21:105.")].

A review on the complex interplay between protein coronas (PCs), nanoparticles (NPs), and exosomes (EVs) describes the PC formation and how it influences biodistribution, cellular uptake, as well as clearance of NPs. Since EVs can also acquire PCs, an understanding of the PC formation on EVs can provide insights into optimizing their use in drug delivery and diagnostics [[24](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR24 "Heidarzadeh M, Zarebkohan A, Rahbarghazi R, Sokullu E. Protein corona and exosomes: new challenges and prospects. Cell Commun Signal. 2023;21:64.")].

**Research articles**:

Risner and colleagues investigated the impact of EV generation on intercellular trafficking and cell survival. Through the inhibition of neutral sphingomyelinase (nSMAse), the inhibition of nSMAse shifted the size of the generated EVs to larger particles. Those particles exhibit features of apoptotic bodies which promote neurodegeneration [[25](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR25 "Risner ML, Ribeiro M, McGrady NR, Kagitapalli BS, Chamling X, Zack DJ, Calkins DJ. Neutral sphingomyelinase inhibition promotes local and network degeneration in vitro and in vivo. Cell Commun Signal. 2023;21:305.")].

Outer membrane vesicles (OMVs) from the probiotic *Escherichia coli* O83 (EcO83) have been shown to reduce allergic airways inflammation. In this study by Schmid et al. the content of isolated EcO83-OMVs was analyzed and the immunomodulatory effects of EcO83-OMV were investigated in experimental infection models in cells as well as in mice. The data of the study show that intranasal administration of EcO83-OMVs leads to an anti-allergic response through the inhibition of airway hyperresponsiveness, and decreased airway eosinophilia, Th2 cytokine production and mucus secretion, suggesting that bacterial OMVs might represent a prophylactic treatment strategy to reduce the severity of allergic reactions [[26](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR26 "Schmid AM, Razim A, Wysmołek M, Kerekes D, Haunstetter M, Kohl P, Brazhnikov G, Geissler N, Thaler M, Krčmářová E, et al. Extracellular vesicles of the probiotic bacteria E. coli O83 activate innate immunity and prevent allergy in mice. Cell Commun Signal. 2023;21:297.")].

Dermal mesenchymal stem cell (DMSC)-derived EVs require peroxiredoxin II (Prx II) expression. In this report, the authors analyzed the role of Prx II in the generation of EVs and found that depletion of Prx II significantly reduced the number of exosomes produced by DMSCs. Instead, significantly more multivesicular bodies (MVBs) were detected in the cytosol, which are described as EV precursors. The authors concluded that Prx II is involved in the regulation of EV secretion [[27](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR27 "Han YH, Mao YY, Lee KH, Cho HJ, Yu NN, Xing XY, Wang AG, Jin MH, Hong KS, Sun HN, Kwon T. Peroxiredoxin II regulates exosome secretion from dermal mesenchymal stem cells through the ISGylation signaling pathway. Cell Commun Signal. 2023;21:296.")].

Zhu et al. showed that filopodia of macrophages produce filopodial tip vesicles (FTVs), which deliver signaling molecules to fibroblasts. They found that FTVs transport IL-11, which is implicated in fibroblast transdifferentiation contributing to renal interstitial fibrosis [[28](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR28 "Zhu X, Zhao Y, Liu Y, Shi W, Yang J, Liu Z, Zhang X. Macrophages release IL11-containing filopodial tip vesicles and contribute to renal interstitial inflammation. Cell Commun Signal. 2023;21:293.")].

Since EVs originating from cells of the central nervous system (CNS) can be detected in the blood stream, this study by Brahmer et al. investigates the use of multiplex bead-based flow cytometry to determine the phenotypic profiles of CNS-derived EVs from patients with glioblastoma, multiple sclerosis and Alzheimer’s disease and report a set of markers with potential as early diagnostic marker for neurogenerative diseases and tumors in the brain [[29](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR29 "Brahmer A, Geiß C, Lygeraki A, Neuberger E, Tzaridis T, Nguyen TT, Luessi F, Régnier-Vigouroux A, Hartmann G, Simon P, et al. Assessment of technical and clinical utility of a bead-based flow cytometry platform for multiparametric phenotyping of CNS-derived extracellular vesicles. Cell Commun Signal. 2023;21:276.")].

Long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) have been frequently described to be associated with exosomes of EVs. This publication reports that lncRNA Mir100hg is upregulated in lung cancer stem cells and propose that the RNA can be transported via exosomes (EVs) to non-stemness cancer cells. The authors further show how miR100hg affects miR-15a-5p and miR-31-5p, and link this to an increase of the metastatic state of the target cells [[30](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR30 "Shi L, Li B, Zhang Y, Chen Y, Tan J, Chen Y, Li J, Xiang M, Xing HR, Wang J. Exosomal lncRNA Mir100hg derived from cancer stem cells enhance glycolysis and promote metastasis of lung adenocarcinoma through mircroRNA-15a-5p/31-5p. Cell Commun Signal. 2023;21:248.")].

The implication of extracellular vesicles in the embryo-maternal communication in the *Sus scrofa* model was investigated by Guzewska and colleagues. They found that EVs are generated and exchanged at the embryo-maternal interface in the early phase of pregnancy leading to a transcriptional reprogramming of the endometrium [[31](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR31 "Guzewska MM, Myszczynski K, Heifetz Y, Kaczmarek MM. Embryonic signals mediate extracellular vesicle biogenesis and trafficking at the embryo-maternal interface. Cell Commun Signal. 2023;21:210.")].

This study addresses the impact of bone mesenchymal stem cell (bMSCs)-derived EVs on the degradation of endothelial glycocalyx and vascular leakage induced by LPS, and discuss their potential as therapeutic agents. The authors further investigate the pathways affected by EVs within the endothelial cells which include transfer of let7 5p miRNA, associated with a modulation of *ABL2* gene expressionp38 MAPK expression inhibition and, ultimately, promoting an inflammatory response [[32](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR32 "Li Z, Xu Y, Lu S, Gao Y, Deng Y. Bone mesenchymal stem cell extracellular vesicles delivered miR let-7-5p alleviate endothelial glycocalyx degradation and leakage via targeting ABL2. Cell Commun Signal. 2023;21:205.")].

Irmer et al. investigated the role of EVs in delivery of receptors in breast cancer. It was demonstrated that ROR1 and ROR2 receptors can be transported via EVs to ROR-negative cells, which enhance cancer cell migration and invasion. The paper further discusses how EV-associated ROR1/2 contributes to tumor progression by attaching to cancer cell surfaces and activating RhoA signaling, promoting aggressive phenotypes in breast cancer [[33](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR33 "Irmer B, Efing J, Reitnauer LE, Angenendt A, Heinrichs S, Schubert A, Schulz M, Binder C, Tio J, Hansen U, et al. Extracellular vesicle-associated tyrosine kinase-like orphan receptors ROR1 and ROR2 promote breast cancer progression. Cell Commun Signal. 2023;21:171.")].

In the publication by Buchacher et al., the role of EVs from *Bacillus cereus* in transporting enterotoxins was investigated. The authors describe how EVs efficiently transport Nhe (Non-hemolytic enterotoxin) and Hbl (Hemolysin BL), and discuss their contribution to the bacterium’s pathogenicity [[34](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR34 "Buchacher T, Digruber A, Kranzler M, Del Favero G, Ehling-Schulz M. Bacillus cereus extracellular vesicles act as shuttles for biologically active multicomponent enterotoxins. Cell Commun Signal. 2023;21:112.")].

How bacterial extracellular vesicles from sepsis-associated pathogens impact the vascular protective factor RNase1 was analyzed by Laakmann and colleagues. This report shows that OMVs from the gram-negative bacteria *Escherichia coli*, *Klebsiella pneumoniae*, and *Salmonella enterica* serovar Typhimurium suppress RNase1 in human lung endothelial cells through a LPS-induced TLR4-IRAK-1 and p38-mediated signaling pathway. This suppression promotes endothelial inflammation and dysfunction, contributing to sepsis progression [[35](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR35 "Laakmann K, Eckersberg JM, Hapke M, Wiegand M, Bierwagen J, Beinborn I, Preußer C, von Pogge E, Heimerl T, Schmeck B, Jung AL. Bacterial extracellular vesicles repress the vascular protective factor RNase1 in human lung endothelial cells. Cell Commun Signal. 2023;21:111.")].

The impact of OMVs from different pathogens on viral replication in macrophages was investigated in the research publication by Bierwagen et al. They demonstrate that OMVs activate Toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4) and its adaptor protein TRIF in macrophages, which leads to the induction of antiviral responses [[36](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR36 "Bierwagen J, Wiegand M, Laakmann K, Danov O, Limburg H, Herbel SM, Heimerl T, Dorna J, Jonigk D, Preußer C, et al. Bacterial vesicles block viral replication in macrophages via TLR4-TRIF-axis. Cell Commun Signal. 2023;21:65.")].

Sato et al. examined the role of the Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) protein BGLF2 in promoting viral infection through exosomes. The BGLF2 protein is incorporated into exosomes produced by EBV-infected cells. Through the uptake of these exosomes, BGLF2 activates the AP-1 promoter implicated in an enhancement of EBV infectivity [[37](/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01999-8#ref-CR37 "Sato Y, Yaguchi M, Okuno Y, Ishimaru H, Sagou K, Ozaki S, Suzuki T, Inagaki T, Umeda M, Watanabe T, et al. Epstein-Barr virus tegument protein BGLF2 in exosomes released from virus-producing cells facilitates de novo infection. Cell Commun Signal. 2022;20:95.")].

## Data availability

No datasets were generated or analysed during the current study.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Extracellular vesicles (EVs) facilitate essential signaling between cells, conveying complex information that influences cellular behavior and function, impacting both health and disease.
Exosomes are nanosized EVs (30-150 nm) generated from multivesicular bodies, playing critical roles in various physiological processes, including cell signaling and immune responses.
Microvesicles are larger than exosomes (50-1000 nm), formed directly from the plasma membrane, involved in mediating intercellular communication and transferring biomolecular cargo.
EVs efficiently transport proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids, acting as vehicles for bioactive molecules that modulate recipient cell functions and influence physiological processes.
EVs play a pivotal role in tissue regeneration by enhancing cellular repair mechanisms, modulating inflammation, and facilitating communication between damaged tissues and surrounding cells.
EVs exhibit unique properties, such as biocompatibility and targeted delivery capabilities, making them promising candidates for novel drug delivery systems to treat various diseases.
